segments.text: Bacterial and Phage Genetics

Gene Transfer in Prokaryotes
Conjugation
Transformation
Transduction

Transduction
One of the mechanisms by which bacteria transfer genes from one strain to another; donor DNA is packaged within the protein coat of a bacteriophage and transferred to the recipient when the phage particle infects it.  Recipient cells are known as transductants.
Classification of animal viruses

Viruses can self-assemble, and are relatively simple
All contain nucleic acid and protein
Human Immunodeficiency Virus
Virus DNA can be introduced into host cell
Life Cycle of Virulent Phage

Virulent phage
Viruses that always enter the lytic cycle, multiply rapidly and kill the host

Colonies              Plaques
Bacteria                                                                                     Viruses feeding on bacteria

Temperate phage
Viruses that can either enter the lytic cycle or the lysogenic cycle
Life Cycle of Temperate Phage

Lysogeny
The integration of bacteriophage DNA into the host

Transduction Variations
Generalized-transfers any part of bacterial chromosome
Specialized-transfers only a specific part of a bacterial chromosome
Generalized Transduction

Bacteriophage P22
First used in generalized transduction
Infects Salmonella
Integrates into the bacterial 	chromosome

Bacterophage P1
Used in generalized transduction
Does not integrate into the bacterial 	chromosome.
Replicates as a 	plasmid.
Encodes Cre recombinase-lox sites
Example of modern use of P1:Tissue specific knockout in mouse
Production of Specialized Transductants

Bacteriophage l
Basic structure of l; lytic cycle and lysogen; the function of an anti-erminator; cro, cI, cII and the lysis-lysogeny switch; DNA replication and packaging-spontaneous assembly = thermodynamics are your friend.
Specialized Transduction

Viral Genetics
Infection of susceptible host with at least two virus particles at one time.

Example of Viral Phenotypes:
Colony morphology
Host range
Note: this is a darkfield image, so cloudy and clear are really the opposite

Crosses between viral strains can be used to:
map distances between genes
test if mutations are allelic (complementation assays)
map distance between mutations in the same gene
There are many known T-series bacteriophage

Viral genes:T2
Gene 1, two alleles:
h+   wild-type allele can infect E. coli 		        strain B, but not B/2
h-    mutant allele that can infect E.     	        		coli strain B or B/2
If one infects a mixture of E. coli B and B/2, the mutant allele, h- will infect all the cells, and the plaque will be clear.  Wild type, will only infect  E. coli strain B, and so the plaque will be cloudy.

Viral genes:T2
Gene 2, two alleles
r+    wild-type allele produces small 	  		plaques
r-     mutant allele that is rapid lysis, 			produces large plaques

MOI
Multiplicity Of Infection
Two or more phage must infect one bacteria to have recombination
Genetic cross between viruses

Example of T2 Viral Phenotypes
Genotype	Phenotype
h+ r+  		cloudy small
h+ r-		cloudy large
h- r+		clear small
h- r-		clear large
Note: they are haploid
Note: this is a darkfield image, so cloudy and clear are really the opposite
